comment.body:
it's not all lie, but not all true either. 
stop-hunting is juicing liquidity. rangebounding is very good for that, long vol positions can be kick out, volatility can be suppressed + MMs have a history of manipulating bid/ask, but not in a way that a random yt dude would know. and not all firms do that, sure

comment.body:
mmm, on a weekly-monthly basis.
on an intraday basis, there is no purpose on that.
when you see the change, the effect is already there. 
it doesn't give any valuable additional information that could not be extracted by IV-RV, and momentum metrics, or simply the chart itself.

i said this: technical structure first with contexts assigned to it.
then check the profile only to see how the market initially sees the structure in the present context/state

thats where i take my ideas from

comment.body:
but it is easy:
where is the highest gamma and vega? at ATM 
where is the highest speed, vomma and charm? at OTM (and ITM, but speed is negative, charm is positive)
+ITM has intrinsic value that is not exposed to theta and vega.

so ATM along with ITM legs when expect faster, more convex move and
and OTM when you expect longer-term moves + they are cheap... lower conviction, to add to a futures position

comment.body:
FVG = fair value gap. 

A Fair Value Gap is a three-candle pattern that represents a market inefficiency or imbalance, where price moves so aggressively in one direction that it leaves an untraded "gap" or void between the wick (or extreme) of the first candle and the wick (or extreme) of the third candle, with little to no overlap from the surrounding candles.This gap occurs because buying and selling pressure was extremely lopsided during a rapid impulsive move, creating an area of inefficient pricing that the market often returns to later to "fill" or rebalance before continuing in the original direction (or sometimes reversing).
comment.raw_body:
